title: Britain Since 1945
overview:
After winning the Second World War, Britain faced enormous challenges of rebuilding and social reform. This period saw the creation of the welfare state, the National Health Service, decolonisation of the British Empire, waves of immigration, social change in the 1960s, economic troubles in the 1970s, the Thatcher years, conflicts in Northern Ireland, joining the European Economic Community, devolution, and major political changes through to the present day.

This lesson covers British history from 1945 to 2024, including key Prime Ministers, major reforms, social changes, conflicts, and the development of modern Britain.

study_sections.heading: The Welfare State (1945-1951)
study_sections.content:
Although the UK had won the war, the country was exhausted economically and the people wanted change. During the war, there had been significant reforms to the education system and people now looked for wider social reforms.

In 1945 the British people elected a Labour government. The new Prime Minister was Clement Attlee (1883-1967), who promised to introduce the welfare state outlined in the Beveridge Report. Attlee was Prime Minister from 1945 to 1951 and led the Labour Party for 20 years.

In 1948, Aneurin (Nye) Bevan, the Minister for Health, led the establishment of the National Health Service (NHS), which guaranteed a minimum standard of health care for all, free at the point of use. A national system of benefits was also introduced to provide 'social security', so that the population would be protected from the 'cradle to the grave'.

The government took into public ownership (nationalised) major industries including the railways, coal mines and gas, water and electricity supplies.

William Beveridge (1879-1963), later Lord Beveridge, was a British economist who wrote the 1942 Beveridge Report. It recommended that the government should find ways of fighting the five 'Giant Evils' of Want, Disease, Ignorance, Squalor and Idleness, and provided the basis of the modern welfare state.

R A Butler (1902-82), later Lord Butler, oversaw the introduction of the Education Act 1944 (often called 'The Butler Act'), which introduced free secondary education in England and Wales.

study_sections.heading: Decolonisation and the Post-War World
study_sections.content:
Another aspect of change was self-government for former colonies. In 1947, independence was granted to nine countries, including India, Pakistan and Ceylon (now Sri Lanka). Other colonies in Africa, the Caribbean and the Pacific achieved independence over the next 20 years.

The UK developed its own atomic bomb and joined the new North Atlantic Treaty Organization (NATO), an alliance of nations set up to resist the perceived threat of invasion by the Soviet Union and its allies.

Britain had a Conservative government from 1951 to 1964. The 1950s were a period of economic recovery after the war and increasing prosperity for working people. The Prime Minister of the day, Harold Macmillan, was famous for his 'wind of change' speech about decolonisation and independence for the countries of the Empire.

study_sections.heading: Migration in Post-War Britain
study_sections.content:
Rebuilding Britain after the Second World War was a huge task. There were labour shortages and the British government encouraged workers from Ireland and other parts of Europe to come to the UK and help with the reconstruction. In 1948, people from the West Indies were also invited to come and work.

During the 1950s, there was still a shortage of labour in the UK. Further immigration was therefore encouraged for economic reasons, and many industries advertised for workers from overseas. For example, centres were set up in the West Indies to recruit people to drive buses. Textile and engineering firms from the north of England and the Midlands sent agents to India and Pakistan to find workers.

For about 25 years, people from the West Indies, India, Pakistan and (later) Bangladesh travelled to work and settle in Britain. The number of people migrating fell in the late 1960s because the government passed new laws to restrict immigration to Britain. During the early 1970s, Britain admitted 28,000 people of Indian origin who had been forced to leave Uganda.

study_sections.heading: Social Change in the 1960s
study_sections.content:
The decade of the 1960s was a period of significant social change. It was known as 'the Swinging Sixties'. There was growth in British fashion, cinema and popular music. Two well-known pop music groups at the time were The Beatles and The Rolling Stones. People started to become better off and many bought cars and other consumer goods.

It was also a time when social laws were liberalised, for example in relation to divorce and to abortion in England, Wales and Scotland. The position of women in the workplace also improved. It was quite common at the time for employers to ask women to leave their jobs when they got married, but Parliament passed new laws giving women the right to equal pay and made it illegal for employers to discriminate against women because of their gender.

The 1960s was also a time of technological progress. Britain and France developed the world's only supersonic commercial airliner, Concorde, which first flew in 1969 and began carrying passengers in 1976. Concorde was retired from service in 2003. New styles of architecture, including high-rise buildings and the use of concrete and steel, became common.

study_sections.heading: Problems in the Economy in the 1970s
study_sections.content:
In the late 1970s, the post-war economic boom came to an end. Prices of goods and raw materials began to rise sharply and the exchange rate between the pound and other currencies was unstable. This caused problems with the 'balance of payments': imports of goods were valued at more than the price paid for exports.

Many industries and services were affected by strikes and this caused problems between the trade unions and the government. People began to argue that the unions were too powerful and that their activities were harming the UK.

The 1970s were also a time of serious unrest in Northern Ireland. In 1972, the Northern Ireland Parliament was suspended and Northern Ireland was directly ruled by the UK government. Some 3,000 people lost their lives in the decades after 1969 in the violence in Northern Ireland.

study_sections.heading: Europe and the Common Market
study_sections.content: West Germany, France, Belgium, Italy, Luxembourg and the Netherlands formed the European Economic Community (EEC) in 1957. At first the UK did not wish to join the EEC but it eventually did so in 1973. The UK is a full member of the European Union but does not use the Euro currency.

study_sections.heading: Conservative Government 1979-1997
study_sections.content:
Margaret Thatcher (1925-2013) became the first woman Prime Minister of the UK in 1979. She was the longest-serving Prime Minister of the 20th century, remaining in office until 1990. She was the daughter of a grocer from Grantham in Lincolnshire and trained as a chemist and lawyer.

The Conservative government made structural changes to the economy through the privatisation of nationalised industries and imposed legal controls on trade union powers. Deregulation saw a great increase in the role of the City of London as an international centre for investments, insurance and other financial services. Traditional industries, such as shipbuilding and coal mining, declined.

In 1982, Argentina invaded the Falkland Islands, a British overseas territory in the South Atlantic. A naval taskforce was sent from the UK and military action led to the recovery of the islands.

John Major was Prime Minister after Mrs Thatcher, and helped establish the Northern Ireland peace process.

study_sections.heading: Labour Government 1997-2010
study_sections.content:
In 1997 the Labour Party led by Tony Blair was elected. The Blair government introduced a Scottish Parliament and a Welsh Assembly. The Scottish Parliament has substantial powers to legislate. The Welsh Assembly was given fewer legislative powers but considerable control over public services.

In Northern Ireland, the Blair government was able to build on the peace process, resulting in the Good Friday Agreement signed in 1998. The Northern Ireland Assembly was elected in 1999 but suspended in 2002. It was not reinstated until 2007. Most paramilitary groups in Northern Ireland have decommissioned their arms and are inactive.

Gordon Brown took over as Prime Minister in 2007.

Throughout the 1990s and 2000s, Britain played a leading role in coalition forces, including operations in Afghanistan and Iraq. British combat troops left Iraq in 2009.

study_sections.heading: Coalition Government 2010-2015
study_sections.content: In May 2010, and for the first time in the UK since February 1974, no political party won an overall majority in the General Election. The Conservative and Liberal Democrat parties formed a coalition and the leader of the Conservative Party, David Cameron, became Prime Minister.

study_sections.heading: Conservative Government 2015-2024
study_sections.content:
The Conservative Government, which governed the United Kingdom from 2015 to 2024, saw multiple prime ministers, including David Cameron, Theresa May, Boris Johnson, Liz Truss, and Rishi Sunak.

During this period, significant events included the 2016 EU membership referendum (Brexit), leadership changes, and handling of the COVID-19 pandemic. The UK left the European Union on 31 January 2020.

Rishi Sunak called for a general election on 4 July 2024. The Conservative Party's popularity declined during this time.

study_sections.heading: Labour Government from 2024
study_sections.content: The Labour party, led by Keir Starmer, won the majority in the 2024 general election and formed the government. This ended 14 years of Conservative government.

key_facts: In 1945 the British people elected a Labour government led by Clement Attlee.
Clement Attlee was Prime Minister from 1945 to 1951.
In 1948, the National Health Service (NHS) was established by Aneurin (Nye) Bevan.
The NHS guaranteed a minimum standard of health care for all, free at the point of use.
The Beveridge Report (1942) recommended fighting the five 'Giant Evils': Want, Disease, Ignorance, Squalor and Idleness.
The Attlee government nationalised the railways, coal mines and gas, water and electricity supplies.
The Education Act 1944 (Butler Act) introduced free secondary education in England and Wales.
In 1947, independence was granted to India, Pakistan and Ceylon (now Sri Lanka).
The UK developed its own atomic bomb and joined NATO.
Britain had a Conservative government from 1951 to 1964.
Harold Macmillan gave the 'wind of change' speech about decolonisation.
In 1948, people from the West Indies were invited to come and work in the UK.
Workers from India, Pakistan and Bangladesh came to work in Britain for about 25 years.
The 1960s was known as 'the Swinging Sixties'.
The Beatles and The Rolling Stones were well-known pop music groups in the 1960s.
Social laws were liberalised in the 1960s in relation to divorce and abortion.
New laws gave women the right to equal pay and made gender discrimination illegal.
Concorde first flew in 1969 and began carrying passengers in 1976.
Concorde was retired from service in 2003.
In 1972, the Northern Ireland Parliament was suspended.
Some 3,000 people lost their lives in the decades after 1969 in Northern Ireland violence.
The European Economic Community (EEC) was formed in 1957.
The UK joined the EEC in 1973.
Margaret Thatcher became the first woman Prime Minister in 1979.
Margaret Thatcher was Prime Minister from 1979 to 1990.
She was the longest-serving Prime Minister of the 20th century.
In 1982, Argentina invaded the Falkland Islands.
The UK sent a naval taskforce to recover the Falkland Islands.
John Major helped establish the Northern Ireland peace process.
In 1997 the Labour Party led by Tony Blair was elected.
The Blair government introduced a Scottish Parliament and a Welsh Assembly.
The Good Friday Agreement was signed in 1998.
The Northern Ireland Assembly was elected in 1999.
Gordon Brown became Prime Minister in 2007.
British combat troops left Iraq in 2009.
In 2010, the Conservative and Liberal Democrat parties formed a coalition.
David Cameron became Prime Minister in 2010.
Conservative governments from 2015-2024 included David Cameron, Theresa May, Boris Johnson, Liz Truss, and Rishi Sunak.
The 2016 EU membership referendum resulted in Brexit.
The UK left the European Union on 31 January 2020.
COVID-19 pandemic occurred during the Conservative government.
General election was held on 4 July 2024.
Labour party led by Keir Starmer won the 2024 general election.
memory_hook: Remember post-war Britain with 'WELFARE': Welfare state created (Attlee 1945), Empire decolonised (India 1947), Labour reforms (NHS 1948), Falklands War (1982), Attlee to Thatcher to Blair, Reform continues (devolution 1997), End of EU membership (Brexit 2020).

questions.prompt: Who was Prime Minister in 1945?
questions.explanation: Clement Attlee led the Labour government elected in 1945.

questions.prompt: When was the National Health Service (NHS) established?
questions.explanation: The NHS was established in 1948 by Aneurin (Nye) Bevan.

questions.prompt: What did the NHS guarantee?
options: A minimum standard of health care for all, free at the point of use
questions.explanation: The NHS guaranteed minimum health care for all, free at the point of use.

questions.prompt: Who wrote the Beveridge Report?
questions.explanation: William Beveridge wrote the 1942 report that formed the basis of the welfare state.

questions.prompt: What were the five 'Giant Evils' in the Beveridge Report?
options: Want, Disease, Ignorance, Squalor and Idleness
Poverty, War, Hunger, Crime and Unemployment
Racism, Sexism, Classism, Ageism and Discrimination
Inequality, Injustice, Intolerance, Illiteracy and Illness
questions.explanation: The Beveridge Report identified these five evils the government should fight.

questions.prompt: What did the Attlee government nationalise?
options: Railways, coal mines and gas, water and electricity supplies
questions.explanation: The government took these major industries into public ownership.

questions.prompt: When did India gain independence?
questions.explanation: Independence was granted to India, Pakistan and Ceylon in 1947.

questions.prompt: What alliance did the UK join to resist the Soviet Union?
questions.explanation: The UK joined the North Atlantic Treaty Organization (NATO).

questions.prompt: Who was Prime Minister during the 'wind of change' speech?
questions.explanation: Harold Macmillan gave the famous 'wind of change' speech about decolonisation.

questions.prompt: When were people from the West Indies first invited to work in the UK?
questions.explanation: In 1948, people from the West Indies were invited to come and work.

questions.prompt: What was the 1960s decade known as?
questions.explanation: The 1960s was a period of significant social change known as 'the Swinging Sixties'.

questions.prompt: Which pop groups were well-known in the 1960s?
options: The Beatles and The Rolling Stones
questions.explanation: The Beatles and The Rolling Stones were famous 1960s groups.

questions.prompt: When did Concorde first fly?
questions.explanation: Concorde first flew in 1969 and began carrying passengers in 1976.

questions.prompt: When was Concorde retired from service?
questions.explanation: Concorde was retired from service in 2003.

questions.prompt: When was the Northern Ireland Parliament suspended?
questions.explanation: In 1972, the Northern Ireland Parliament was suspended.

questions.prompt: When did the UK join the EEC?
questions.explanation: The UK joined the European Economic Community in 1973.

questions.prompt: Who was the first woman Prime Minister of the UK?
questions.explanation: Margaret Thatcher became the first woman PM in 1979.

questions.prompt: When did Margaret Thatcher become Prime Minister?
questions.explanation: Margaret Thatcher became Prime Minister in 1979.

questions.prompt: How long was Margaret Thatcher Prime Minister?
questions.explanation: She was Prime Minister from 1979 to 1990, the longest-serving PM of the 20th century.

questions.prompt: When did Argentina invade the Falkland Islands?
questions.explanation: Argentina invaded the Falkland Islands in 1982.

questions.prompt: Who was Prime Minister in 1997?
questions.explanation: Tony Blair led the Labour Party to victory in 1997.

questions.prompt: When was the Good Friday Agreement signed?
questions.explanation: The Good Friday Agreement was signed in 1998.

questions.prompt: Who became Prime Minister in 2007?
questions.explanation: Gordon Brown took over as Prime Minister in 2007.

questions.prompt: When did British combat troops leave Iraq?
questions.explanation: British combat troops left Iraq in 2009.

questions.prompt: When was a coalition government formed?
questions.explanation: In 2010, no party won an overall majority, so a coalition was formed.

questions.prompt: Which parties formed the 2010 coalition?
questions.explanation: The Conservative and Liberal Democrat parties formed a coalition in 2010.

questions.prompt: When did the UK leave the European Union?
questions.explanation: The UK left the EU on 31 January 2020.

questions.prompt: When was the general election that ended Conservative rule?
questions.explanation: The general election was held on 4 July 2024.

questions.prompt: Who led Labour to victory in 2024?
questions.explanation: Keir Starmer led the Labour party to victory in 2024.
